setting.note: Mwangi's gritty novel of post-independence Nairobi follows Ben, a cashiered ex-soldier drifting through casual labour, cheap drink and cheaper rooms in the raw underside of the booming city. The real Nairobi is its stage: the bars and flophouses of the notorious River Road, the high-rise construction site where Ben sweats to raise a tower he will never enter, the shanty rooms of the poor, and the gleaming modern centre going up on their backs.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Meja Mwangi, first published 1976. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Going Down River Road, Meja Mwangi (1976). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The red-brown of dust, brick and cheap liquor, the colour of the raw River Road underside of the boom city. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
